verses.text: Give ear, O ye heavens; let the earth hear. My doctrine shall drop as the rain. I will publish the name of the LORD: ascribe ye greatness unto our God. He is the Rock, His work is perfect: all His ways are judgment: a God of truth without iniquity, just and right is He. Guidance: The song opens with heaven and earth as audience. God is called the Rock — the central metaphor of the chapter, repeated four times. Perfect work, just ways, truth without iniquity. The song will accuse the people; first it establishes that the accuser has clean hands.

They have corrupted themselves, a perverse and crooked generation. Do ye thus requite the LORD, O foolish people? Is not He thy father that hath bought thee? Remember the days of old; ask thy father, he will shew thee. When the most High divided to the nations their inheritance, He set the bounds of the people according to the number of the children of Israel. For the LORD's portion is His people; Jacob is the lot of His inheritance. Guidance: God set the maps of nations with Israel in mind. The Almighty drew the borders of the world thinking of His people. The LORD's PORTION is His people — He chose a family as His own treasure when He could have any nation in His pocket.

He found him in a desert land, in the waste howling wilderness; He kept him as the apple of His eye. As an eagle stirreth up her nest, fluttereth over her young, beareth them on her wings — so the LORD alone did lead him. He made him ride on high places; honey out of the rock, oil out of the flinty rock; butter, milk, fat of lambs, rams of Bashan, the pure blood of the grape. Guidance: An eagle teaching her chick to fly stirs the nest, then catches the falling one on her wings. That is God with Israel — He stirred them out of safety and caught them on the way down. The desert became a feast: honey out of rock, oil out of flint. He fed them what only God could squeeze from stone.

But Jeshurun waxed fat, and kicked: thou art waxen fat, grown thick, covered with fatness; then he forsook God which made him, lightly esteemed the Rock of his salvation. They provoked Him to jealousy with strange gods, sacrificed unto devils, not to God; to gods whom they knew not. Of the Rock that begat thee thou art unmindful, hast forgotten God that formed thee. Guidance: Jeshurun — the 'upright one' — got rich and kicked the One who fed him. The Rock that begat you, forgotten. New gods that just showed up get the sacrifices the old God used to receive. Prosperity, not poverty, is the deepest test of faith.

When the LORD saw it, He abhorred them. He said, I will hide my face from them; I will see what their end shall be: a froward generation, children in whom is no faith. They have moved Me to jealousy with that which is not God; I will move them to jealousy with those which are not a people. A fire is kindled in mine anger. They shall be burnt with hunger; the teeth of beasts; the sword without, terror within, shall destroy young and old together. Guidance: God's withdrawal is reciprocal — they provoked Him with non-gods, He provokes them with a non-people. The sword takes the young and the old together; terror lives inside the house. The chapter does not blink at how covenant breakage looks from inside the city walls.

I would have scattered them and made the remembrance of them cease — were it not that I feared the wrath of the enemy, lest their adversaries should say, Our hand is high, and the LORD hath not done all this. They are a nation void of counsel. O that they would consider their latter end! How should one chase a thousand, except their Rock had sold them? For their rock is not as our Rock, even our enemies themselves being judges. Their vine is of Sodom; their wine is the poison of dragons. Guidance: God restrains His anger so His name is not slandered by gloating enemies. And then a quiet comparison: even pagan enemies, if honest, would admit their rock is not the LORD. Israel's losses came not from the strength of foreign gods but from the LORD's withdrawal.

Is not this laid up in store with Me, sealed up among My treasures? To Me belongeth vengeance, and recompence; their foot shall slide in due time: for the day of their calamity is at hand. For the LORD shall judge His people, repent Himself for His servants, when He seeth their power is gone, none shut up, or left. He shall say, Where are their gods, their rock in whom they trusted? let them rise up and help you. Guidance: God's vengeance is filed away, never forgotten. And when the people hit bottom — power gone, no helpers left — He turns. He repents not from His word but from continued discipline. The God who lets you fall is the God who catches you at the end of falling. Where are your gods? — the rhetorical question waiting for an absent answer.

See now that I, even I, am He, there is no god with Me: I kill, I make alive; I wound, I heal: neither is there any that can deliver out of My hand. I lift up My hand to heaven, and say, I live for ever. If I whet My glittering sword, I will render vengeance to Mine enemies, reward them that hate Me. Rejoice, O ye nations, with His people: for He will avenge the blood of His servants, will be merciful unto His land, and to His people. Guidance: 'I, even I, am He' — God's signature on the verse Paul will read in Romans 14. The kill-and-make-alive verb pair is His alone. And the final note is mercy: the song ends with nations rejoicing with Israel. Even the song of judgment closes on the inclusion of the world in God's healing.

Moses came and spake all the words of this song in the ears of the people, he, and Hoshea son of Nun. Moses said: Set your hearts unto all the words which I testify among you this day, which ye shall command your children to observe to do, all the words of this law. For it is not a vain thing for you; because it is your life: through this thing ye shall prolong your days in the land. Guidance: The song over, Moses turns to prose: this is not a vain thing — it IS your life. Not a means to life. Not a help to life. Your life. Take it seriously the way a heart takes blood seriously. There is no Israel without this word.

For it is not a vain thing for you; because it is your life: through this thing ye shall prolong your days in the land. Guidance: The song over, Moses turns to prose: this is not a vain thing — it IS your life. Not a means to life. Not a help to life. Your life. Take it seriously the way a heart takes blood seriously. There is no Israel without this word.

The LORD spake unto Moses that selfsame day: Get thee up into mount Nebo, behold the land of Canaan, which I give unto the children of Israel; die in the mount, be gathered unto thy people; as Aaron thy brother died in mount Hor: because ye trespassed against Me at the waters of Meribah-Kadesh, ye sanctified Me not in the midst of the children of Israel. Yet thou shalt see the land before thee; but thou shalt not go thither. Guidance: Meribah comes back at the end. The single moment of striking the rock follows Moses all the way to the mountain. God lets him see the land — a mercy — but not enter. The most sober end the Torah will ever record: the prophet climbs to die, alone with God on a peak.
